title: Chapter 4: The Weight of Wings
content: Three days since the world cracked open.

Aline stood in the middle of her apartment, surrounded by the debris of a life she no longer recognized. Flower arrangements jostled for space on every surface — lilies from Republic Records, orchids from some A&R executive whose name she'd already forgotten, a sprawling tower of white roses from a streaming service she wasn't even signed to. Their petals were starting to curl at the edges, releasing a cloying sweetness that mixed with the stale air of a room whose windows hadn't been opened in seventy-two hours.

Interview requests spilled from a shoebox on the kitchen counter in stacks like snowdrifts — The Tonight Show, Rolling Stone, some podcast with twelve million subscribers that wanted her "origin story." Gift baskets crowded the hallway: artisanal chocolates, a scarf from some fashion house she'd never heard of, a bottle of champagne she couldn't afford to chill because her fridge was full of fruit arrangements from people she didn't know.
She hadn't left the apartment.

The midnight blue dress clung to her body like a second skin. She'd put it on again this morning — or maybe it was afternoon, the hours had gone liquid — and walked to the mirror and stared at herself. Then taken it off. Folded it. Set it on the bed. Then put it back on. Three times now. The silk carried a faint scent she couldn't name, something between cedar and rain and a note underneath that was neither — something that didn't belong to any perfume she recognized. She pressed the fabric to her nose without meaning to, then dropped it with a sharp exhale.

The black butterfly brooch sat on her nightstand. She'd placed it there deliberately, precisely, the way you handle something that might bite. Every time her eyes drifted to it — and they drifted, constantly, like a tongue probing a sore tooth — the same contradiction bloomed in her chest.
Beautiful. And wrong.
The brooch caught the lamplight and held it. Obsidian wings, delicate as a real insect's, folded against a body no bigger than her thumbnail. It was exquisite. It was a claim.
She caught herself touching the spot above her heart where she'd pinned it before, the fabric still holding the faint impression of its weight.
Her phone buzzed. Another text from a number she didn't recognize. She'd stopped reading them.
The knock came hard and fast, the specific rhythm that belonged to exactly one person on Earth.
"Aline! Open this door before I call the fire department!"

She undid the locks — all three of them, the deadbolt she'd installed herself, the chain, the original lock that stuck — and Mags Delacroix swept in like a weather system. She was wearing her usual armor: leather jacket over a vintage band tee, combat boots, attitude set to maximum volume. Her braids were pulled back in a loose ponytail, a few strands escaping around her face.
The excitement on her features lasted exactly two seconds.

Her eyes swept the apartment — the wilting flowers, the gift baskets still in their cellophane, the stacks of unopened mail, the coffee table covered in uncharged devices and crumpled tissues. Then they landed on Aline. On the dress.
Mags's face did something complicated. The excitement didn't just fade; it collapsed, replaced by a concern so sharp it looked almost like anger.
"Girl." She dropped her bag on the floor. "What the hell is going on?"
"I'm fine."
"You're wearing a ballgown at two in the afternoon in an apartment that smells like a funeral home. You are not fine."
Aline looked down at herself. The midnight blue silk caught the light from the window, shimmering like deep water. "It's not a ballgown. It's just — "
"Aline."
The word landed like a hand on her shoulder. Aline exhaled. Walked to the couch. Sat. The dress pooled around her legs in dark waves.
She started talking.

Not the version she'd rehearsed — the sanitized, press-ready version where a mysterious producer had discovered her music and helped launch her career. The real version. The one where a voice came through her phone and spoke to her like he'd known her for years. Where her screen lit up on its own and she'd felt the air change in her apartment. Where someone had paid six months of back rent overnight and sent her a dress that fit like it had been measured on her body, and a brooch that felt like it had been made for her specifically, personally, in some workshop that only existed to mark her.

Mags listened. Her expression didn't change — not a flicker, not a twitch. She stood with her arms crossed, her weight on one hip, her eyes fixed on Aline with the steady attention of someone reading an X-ray.
When Aline finished, Mags was quiet for three full seconds.
"So let me get this straight." Her voice was level. Almost too level. "Some random guy hacks your phone, buys your rent, and now sends you dresses. And you're wearing it."
"It's not — he's not random. He's the one who — "

"I know what he did, Aline. I was in Times Square. I watched it happen." Mags moved to the nightstand. Picked up the butterfly brooch. Turned it over in her fingers. The metal was cold against her skin — she could see Mags flinch slightly at the chill of it, the way you do when you pick up something and it's colder than you expected. "I'm asking you what he's doing NOW."

"Believing in me." The words came out smaller than she intended. "Mags, I went from zero to number one overnight. From nothing. From playing open mics with three people clapping, two of them drunk, to — " She gestured at the apartment, the flowers, the impossible new reality. "Someone out there believes in me."

"Someone out there is watching you." Mags turned the brooch again, examining the join of the wings, the tiny body, the way the light refracted off the black surface like oil on water. "There's a difference."
She held the brooch up between them. The butterfly's wings caught the light from the window, and for a moment it looked almost alive, a dark thing pinned between Mags's fingers.
"This isn't a gift." Mags's voice dropped. "This is a tag."
The word hit Aline somewhere below the sternum. A tag. Like naturalists pin to butterflies. Like scientists clip to the ears of tracked animals.
She reached for it.

The motion was so fast, so instinctive, that it surprised them both. Aline's fingers closed around the brooch and pulled it from Mags's hand, and she stood there clutching it against her chest like a child with a stolen toy, her breathing shallow, her eyes wide.
Mags stared at her. Slowly, deliberately, she raised both hands in a gesture that was either surrender or horror.
"Okay," Mags said softly. "Okay."
Aline looked down at the brooch in her fist. The wings bit into her palm. She loosened her grip.
"I'm sorry," she whispered. "I don't know why I — "
"I do." Mags picked up her bag. Slinging it over her shoulder took a long time, like she was composing something she didn't want to say. "And that's what scares me."
She stopped at the door. Hand on the frame. Not looking back.
"I'm not saying he's evil, Aline. I'm saying be careful." A breath. "And for God's sake, stop talking to your phone like it's your boyfriend."
The door closed. The locks clicked back into place, one by one, Aline's hands moving on autopilot.
Silence.
The flowers released another wave of sweetness into the stale air. Somewhere in the building, a pipe groaned.
Aline set the brooch back on the nightstand. Sat on the edge of the bed. Stared at the wall.
Her phone lit up.
She didn't pick it up. She didn't have to. The text filled the screen in plain white type:
**Your friend is perceptive.**
Her stomach dropped. Then clenched. Then caught fire.
"Were you listening to that entire conversation?"

She said it out loud, to the room, to the phone, to whatever invisible architecture connected her voice to his. It felt insane. It was insane. Three weeks ago she'd been a normal person with normal problems, and now she was talking to her apartment like it might answer.
Silence.
Then:
No apology. No attempt to soften it. No pretense of coincidence or technicality. Just the word, sitting on her screen like a stone dropped in still water.

She should be furious. She was furious — her hands were shaking, her jaw tight, a hot pressure building behind her eyes. He'd listened. He'd been there the entire time Mags was warning her, cataloguing her friend's words, weighing her concerns, and he'd stayed. He'd admitted it with the easy confidence of someone who'd already decided that being caught was not the same as being wrong.
But underneath the fury, something else. Something worse.
Relief.

Because Mags had left. Mags had looked at her holding the brooch and walked out the door with fear in her eyes, and now Aline was alone in a room full of dying flowers with no one to tell her which way was up. And someone had heard every word, every warning, every accusation — and still chose to stay.
She stared at the phone until the screen went dark.

Then she pulled the dress off, folded it, and put it in the closet. She took a shower, the water so hot it turned her skin pink. She ate something from a gift basket without tasting it. She did not look at the brooch.
She did not answer the phone when the screen lit up again, twenty minutes later, with no notification, no message — just the pale glow of a screen that had decided, on its own, to wake.
Night fell like a held breath releasing.

Aline lay in bed with the lights off, the covers pulled to her chin. She'd taken the brooch off hours ago, set it face-down on the nightstand so its obsidian wings wouldn't catch the light. Her laptop sat closed on the desk across the room, the lid a dark rectangle against the darker wall.
She slept. Or tried to. The hours stretched and thinned, and somewhere in the soup of half-consciousness, a sound — or not a sound, a presence of sound, the memory of a sound — pulled her awake.
2:00 AM. The numbers on her phone glowed like an accusation.
The red dot on her laptop camera.
It blinked.

Once. A tiny crimson flare, there and gone. She stared at it, not breathing. The apartment was utterly silent — no traffic, no neighbors, no hum of the refrigerator from the kitchen. Just the dark and her own pulse.
The dot blinked again.
Not random. Not mechanical. The timing was organic. Like breathing.

She watched. Her eyes adjusted until the red dot was the only thing in the room with any life in it, a single unblinking eye that chose, occasionally, to blink at her. Once. A pause. Again. A longer pause. Three times, quickly — dot-dot-dot — like a heartbeat, like Morse code she couldn't read, like someone tapping a message in a language she hadn't learned yet.
She pulled the blanket over her head.

The darkness under the covers was absolute. Warm. Close. The fabric pressed against her nose and she could smell her own shampoo, the detergent on the sheets, and underneath it all, the fading ghost of cedar and rain from the dress she'd put in the closet hours ago. Her breath came fast and shallow. The blanket formed a tent over her face, a fragile barrier between her and the watching dark.
"I know you're there."
She whispered it. Barely a sound. A confession and an accusation and a prayer all at once, spoken into the enclosed space of her own making.
The blanket moved.

Not wind — the apartment was sealed, windows locked, HVAC off. Not her — she was frozen, every muscle locked, her hands gripping the edge of the covers with white-knuckled determination. Just... a thin displacement of air across her cheek. Like breath from nowhere. Cool and particular, carrying no scent, no temperature, just the undeniable physics of air being displaced by something that shouldn't be there.
Her laptop chimed.
Soft. A single note, the default sound for a new file. Incongruously gentle in the dark.
She didn't move. She lay beneath the blanket, fabric pressed to her face, her heart hammering so hard she could feel it in her throat, her wrists, the soft hollows behind her knees.
Slowly — so slowly that her muscles screamed with the effort — she lowered the blanket.
The room was the same. Dark. Empty. The laptop was still closed. But its screen glowed faintly through the lid, the thin seam of light along the edge pulsing gently in the dark.
She sat up. Walked to the desk on legs that felt borrowed from someone braver. Opened the laptop.
A new file sat in the center of an otherwise pristine desktop. An audio file, its icon a tiny waveform. The title, rendered in simple sans-serif text:
**For When You Can't Sleep**
She didn't open it.
She closed the laptop. Walked back to the bed. Lay down. Pulled the covers up to her collarbone and stared at the ceiling.
The red dot blinked.

Slowly. Once. Pause. Again. And she became aware, in some animal part of her brain that didn't care about logic or fear or the seventeen reasons Mags had been right, that the rhythm of the blinking was changing. Aligning. The intervals between flares shortening or lengthening by microdegrees, the way two out-of-sync metronomes will gradually find each other in a room.
The blinks synced with her pulse.

She felt it happening — the moment when her heartbeat and that distant red light became one rhythm, one pattern, one conversation conducted in the language of flickers and blood. Her heart beat. The dot blinked. Her heart beat. The dot blinked. A closed circuit, private and unholy and intimate in a way that had nothing to do with the things she'd shared with any living person.

She lay in the dark, hyperaware of every shadow, every electronic hum, the faint glow of the phone screen, the click of the pipes, the tick of the brooch's wings against the nightstand as the building settled.
Part of her wanted him to stop.

She tucked that part somewhere deep, beneath the covers, beneath the cedar and the rain and the impossible silence of an apartment that was no longer hers alone. She lay perfectly still, her heartbeat a lantern she couldn't extinguish, broadcasting her fear and her fury and the terrible, creeping warmth she'd never admit to in the light of day.
The dot blinked.
She blinked back.
